messages.content: Hey, can we get the Q3 budget planning session started? I know we've been pushing this back but we really need to lock things down before the end of the month.

Agreed, let's do it. I've been waiting on a few numbers from the infrastructure team but I think we have enough to start the high-level conversation. What do you want to tackle first — cloud costs, tooling, or headcount?
Let's start with cloud since that's where we've been bleeding the most. I pulled the Q2 actuals last night and it's not pretty.

Yeah I saw those numbers too. We came in about 22% over forecast on AWS alone. A big chunk of that was the unexpected GPU usage spike in May when the ML team was running those training jobs back to back.
Right, and nobody flagged it until we were already 40K in the hole for that month. That was a mess.

It was. Honestly that was partly a visibility problem — we didn't have good real-time alerting on spend. But we can get to that when we talk about observability tooling. For now, what's the engineering team projecting for GPU usage in Q3?
That's actually something I need to formalize. Can your team put together a proper 6-month cloud cost projection? I want something I can actually put in front of the board.

Yes, engineering will put together the 6-month cloud cost projection. I'll get the ML team and platform team to each contribute their estimates and we'll consolidate it. Give me until end of next week.

Perfect. That's super helpful. Okay so walking through the AWS line items — EC2 is the biggest chunk, then S3, then RDS. The GPU instances are under EC2 but they're basically their own category at this point.
Totally. The p3 and p4 instances are expensive but the team has been making the case that they're necessary for the model iteration speed. If we throttle that, we slow down the product roadmap.
I get that. What's the monthly delta we're looking at if we want to keep the current pace?

Based on what the ML team told me, we need roughly 45K more per month than what's currently allocated to support the training frequency they're planning for Q3. That's on top of the existing AWS budget.
45K a month is significant. That's over half a million annualized if it holds.

It is, but compare it to the cost of slowing down model deployment by two quarters. The product team estimates that delay would cost us more in lost revenue. I know that's a soft number but it's worth factoring in.

Fair point. And the GPU instances are something we've been planning to scale anyway. Okay, I'm going to sign off on this — the $45K monthly AWS budget increase for GPU instances is approved. I'll update the budget model today.
Great, that'll make the ML team very happy. I'll let them know they can proceed with the Q3 training schedule. Thanks for moving on that quickly.
Yeah no problem. I'd rather approve it now than deal with another surprise overrun in August.
Exactly. Okay, should we move to tooling next? I know there's a pending decision on the observability stack that's been dragging on.
Yes please. That decision has been sitting in limbo for like two months. What's the current status?

So we've been evaluating Datadog and New Relic. Both teams have done trials. Infrastructure prefers Datadog, the app engineering team is slightly more split but leans Datadog too. New Relic came back with a pretty aggressive pricing offer last week though.
How aggressive are we talking?

They came down about 18% from their initial quote and threw in some additional user seats. It's a better deal than it was two months ago. But the engineering feedback on the product itself is still that Datadog's APM and log correlation is meaningfully better for our use case.
How much is the price difference now after the New Relic discount?

Datadog is still about 12K more per year. But when you factor in the integration work we'd need to do with New Relic versus just extending our existing Datadog trial setup, the gap closes considerably. Probably under 5K net difference when you include migration effort.
Honestly at under 5K difference in a budget this size, I'd rather just go with the product the engineers actually prefer. Let's settle on Datadog for observability. Get the contract moving.

Perfect. I'll reach out to our Datadog rep and let them know we're moving forward. We had a decent relationship from the trial and I think we can negotiate slightly better terms on the annual commitment.
Good. See if you can get them to throw in some training credits or something. Every bit helps.

Will do. Actually that reminds me — when we were negotiating with New Relic last quarter, they sent us a case of wine as part of the 'relationship building.' Our procurement team had to send it back because of the gift policy. Whole thing was awkward.
Ha, yeah that tracks. Vendors get weird during competitive deals.
The Datadog rep is much more straightforward, which honestly is a good sign. I've found that vendors who are pushy or gimmicky during the sales process tend to be the same way when you need support.
100%. Okay what else is in the tooling bucket for Q3?

We have the CI/CD pipeline tooling refresh — we're evaluating whether to stay on Jenkins or move to GitHub Actions for everything. There's also a request from the security team for a SAST tool, they've been asking for that for a while.
What's the Jenkins vs GitHub Actions cost look like?

GitHub Actions would actually be cheaper given our current GitHub Enterprise contract — we have unused minutes we're not utilizing. Jenkins has hidden costs in maintenance and the time our platform team spends on it. Rough estimate is we save 8-10K annually by moving to GitHub Actions but there's a migration cost upfront.
What's the migration cost?

Platform team estimates about 3 weeks of engineering time to migrate the critical pipelines. At loaded cost that's probably 25-30K one-time. So payback is maybe 3 years. Not the fastest ROI but there are qualitative benefits around developer experience.
That's a longer payback than I'd like for Q3. Can we table the Jenkins migration for now and revisit in Q4 planning?
Yeah that's reasonable. The platform team isn't going to be happy but they'll understand. The SAST tool for security is probably more urgent anyway — we had that finding in the last audit.
Right, we can't keep kicking that one. What's the budget ask for SAST?

Security team is looking at Snyk or Semgrep. Snyk is around 18K annually for our team size, Semgrep has an open-source tier but the enterprise features they want run about 12K. I'd suggest we go with the lower-cost option and revisit if they hit limitations.
Sounds reasonable. Let's go with Semgrep for now. Okay, let's shift to headcount because I know that's the big one for the board.
Yes, and it's the one with the most moving parts. We have three open engineering reqs that are approved in principle but we need to confirm the budget allocation for the full Q3-Q4 run rate.
What are the three reqs?

Senior ML engineer, a platform engineer, and a data engineer. All three are in the pipeline — the ML engineer role we've been trying to fill since February honestly, the market for that profile is brutal right now.
Yeah no kidding. We lost two candidates in the last round to other offers. It's demoralizing for the team too.
The recruiter thinks we need to bump the comp band for the ML engineer role by about 15K to be competitive. That's something we should factor into the budget.
Noted. Let me run that through compensation before we commit to it. What's the total loaded cost for all three reqs if we assume Q3 start dates?

Assuming August starts for all three and using current bands plus benefits and equipment, we're looking at roughly 280K for the second half of the year. That's a partial year number obviously — annualized it's closer to 560K.
Okay that's within the range I had mentally modeled. Good.

There's also the contractor question. We have two contractors supporting the data platform team. Their contracts are up in September and the team wants to convert one of them to FTE. The other one is more of a project-based engagement that can wrap up.
Converting a contractor to FTE mid-year is always a little messy from a budget perspective. What's the cost comparison?

The contractor is billing at $185 an hour through the agency. Converting to FTE at the right comp level would be more cost effective beyond about 7 months. We'd also get more commitment and knowledge retention, which the team really needs right now.
Makes sense. Let's plan for the conversion assuming we can get the comp right. I'll need HR to run the offer numbers.

I'll flag that to the team. Speaking of retention, I know it's slightly off topic but the office space situation has been coming up in engineering team feedback. People are asking about the hybrid setup for Q3 and whether there are plans to change the office footprint.
Yeah that's come up in my conversations too. We're paying for 18,000 square feet and using maybe 40% of it on any given day. That's a real cost we should be looking at.

The lease is up for renewal in 14 months. I know real estate isn't my domain but from a budget perspective, if we downsized to a flex space arrangement we could potentially free up 200-300K annually. Might be worth looping in facilities.
Agreed, but let's keep that out of the Q3 budget discussion for now — it's more of a Q4 and annual planning item. I don't want to open that can of worms today.
Fair enough. Just wanted to flag it since it's real money. Back to Q3 — I think we've covered cloud, tooling, and headcount at a high level. What's missing?
We should probably talk about the contingency buffer. Given what happened in Q2 with the overruns, I want to build in more cushion this time.
What percentage are you thinking? We had 5% last quarter and it got eaten up entirely by the AWS situation plus the unplanned security incident response costs.
I'm thinking 8-10%. It feels high but the variance we've been seeing justifies it.
I'd support 8%. Going to 10% might raise eyebrows from the board since it's a departure from our historical approach. 8% is defensible and still gives us meaningful headroom.
Yeah 8% works. Let's go with that.
Okay. So to summarize where we are: AWS increase approved, Datadog selected, headcount reqs confirmed, Semgrep for security tooling, 8% contingency buffer. That's a solid Q3 framework.
That's a good summary. Are there any other line items I'm missing?

There's a smaller item — the engineering team has been asking for a conference and training budget for Q3. Last year we cut it and it came up repeatedly in the engagement survey as a pain point. It's not a huge number but it matters for morale.
What did we have in previous years?

We allocated 1,500 per engineer for training and conferences. With 22 engineers that's 33K. We cut it to 500 per person in Q2 which saved money but caused some friction. A few people mentioned it in their one-on-ones as a signal about how the company values development.
Let's restore it to 1,200. Not quite back to full but a meaningful step up. I can justify that.
The team will appreciate that. 1,200 per person is enough for one conference or a solid Pluralsight or O'Reilly subscription plus some workshops.
Exactly. Okay I think we have a pretty complete picture now. What do we need to do to get this ready for the board presentation?

I'll need to pull everything together into a clean deck. The board is going to want a one-page budget summary slide with the key numbers and the rationale for the increases. When is the board meeting?
It's Thursday of next week. So we need the materials by Wednesday at the latest, ideally Tuesday to give them time to review.
I can have the board budget slide ready by Monday. That gives you and the CFO time to review it before it goes out to the board members.
Perfect. Finance will prepare the board budget slide by Monday — I'll hold you to that. Make sure it includes the Q3 summary, the AWS rationale, and the headcount plan.
Absolutely. I'll also include a brief comparison to Q2 actuals so the board can see we're being thoughtful about the overrun. Transparency helps there.
Good thinking. One thing I want to avoid is having a repeat of Q1 where we walked into the board meeting and got blindsided by questions we hadn't anticipated.

That was rough. The questions about the CDN spend caught everyone off guard because nobody had thought to include context about the traffic growth driving it. This time let's do a quick pre-read walkthrough with the CFO before the meeting.
Agreed. Schedule something with her for Tuesday afternoon if you can.

Will do. Also — once I have the board slide drafted, can you share the 6-month cloud projection from engineering? That'll be an appendix item but the board members who dig into numbers will want to see it.
Yes, I'll make sure the engineering team has that projection ready in time. It should tie into the board slide narrative.
Great. I think we're in good shape. Anything else before we wrap up?
Just one thing — can you send me a quick recap email of everything we decided today? I want to make sure we're aligned before anything goes into the budget system.
Sure, I'll send that over within the hour. Decisions, budget line items, and action items — all in one place. Thanks for making time for this, I know your calendar is brutal.
Yeah it's been a week. But this was a productive session. Let's plan to sync again once you have the board slide drafted.
Sounds good. Talk soon.
